texts.full_text: moms who love their brown babies, by moms who do the same. Through their posts, our MyBrownBaby bloggers lift the voices of African-American moms looking for the 411/advice/a high-five on everything from pregnancy and childrearing to sex, work, and relationships—all filtered through the lens of the African American experience. View my complete profile Get A MyBrownBaby button here I'M AN ARTIST, AND I'M SENsitive about it. Adding art into your parenting style or sharing that part of you with others can be an enriching and meaningful way to connect on a deeper level—whether through visual arts like painting, drawing; performance such as music, dance; writing poetry, stories—or any other creative outlet. For Black artists especially—the process isn’t just about the art itself but it's deeply rooted in cultural identity. It’s sharing your story with

Hell is now available!War in the West Wacht am Rhein AAR Forums Register Login Photo Gallery Member List Search Calendars FAQ My Profile Inbox Address Book My Subscription My Forums Log Out The Case of Fyodor Krasovsky View related threads: (in this forum | in all forums) Logged in as: Guest Users viewing this topic: none Printable Version All Forums >> [New Releases from Matrix Games] >> Gary Grigsby's War in the East Series >> The Case of Fyodor Krasovsky Username: Date: 03/15/2024 02:45 PM Alright, so I’ve been diving into the Stalingrad scenario in , and I’m stuck on how to handle the AI’s relentless pressure on Krasovsky’s 62nd Army. Historically, his forces were stretched thin, but in the game, the AI seems to exploit this even more. My question: What’s the best strategy to stabilize the southern sector while keeping supply lines intact? I’ve tried reinforcing with reserves, but the AI just keeps hammering with Panzer IVs and StuG IIIs. Any tips on managing attrition without
